# Here is a comparison between the number of students in the top 6 majors (first dict) and the number of students in both of Dr. Hinks's sections:
<img src="https://grahamnoah7777.github.io/grahamnoah7777.github.io-repository2/static/imgs/compare.png" alt="Comparison between top 6 majors and total. "  width="500"/>



Of the 534 students in Dr. Hinks's sections, 357 of them, or 67%, fall into six majors. In order to benefit these students, exercises could be slightly modified to specifically include applications to these majors. Since there are 9 exercises and only six highly-represented majors, it would be feasible to include content from each major among the exercises. Since computer science was not one of the highly represented majors, it is likely that most students taking this course are taking it as either a major requirement, or to learn programming applications to their field of study. Implementing applications for highly represented majors would allow most students to feel more of a connection and interest in exercises, along with giving them practical skills and insight into programming in their area of focus. This would directly benefit the two-thirds of students who are in one of the top six majors in COMP110. The biggest trade-off is that students in under-represented majors might feel less of a connection to the material, since they would feel that the program is not trying to appeal to them as much as to other students. To combat this, the specific implementations of highly-represented majors could be subtle and not require prerequisite knowledge. 
A potential additional exploration would be to find out if different majors could be segmented into related fields of CS application (e.g. business and economics, or environmental science and biology) so that almost every major grouping could have an exercise that implements relevant and interesting elements for their major.
